item.title: Marvel 1943: Rise of Hydra Delayed Again, Won’t Arrive Until 2027 as Amy Hennig’s “Relatively Small” Team is Given “Time to Cook”
item.content:encoded:
Last week, Skydance New Media and Skydance Interactive were officially brought together under one umbrella as Paramount Skydance revealed Paramount Game Studios, with Tony Driscoll running the division and veteran creator Amy Hennig in the creative director's chair. While part of that reveal was that Hennig's Marvel 1943: Rise of Hydra and untitled Star Wars game are still in the works, in an interview with IGN following the reveal, we got a timeline update for Rise of Hydra that you could probably guess, which is that it has, unsurprisingly, been delayed again.

That confirmation comes from Shawn Kittleson, who was named senior vice president, head of creative and production for Paramount Game Studios after being the senior vice president of creative at Skydance Interactive. Kittleson dug a bit into why the game has taken so long to develop, and also set expectations around when we'll hear something about that Star Wars game, which was originally confirmed all the way back in 2022.

On Rise of Hydra, Kittleson said "It's a game. It's happening. It's playable. I've got my SpongeBob controller here, but it's on my hard drive right now. So I can go through and play the build. We're continuing development on it, but Amy and the team have big ambitions for the level of quality that they want to hit."

"They are a relatively small team considering the triple-A quality that they're delivering. What you've seen in previous footage is what the game looks like, and we're making it with a fraction of the resources that you see on other triple-A games of the same type, and really trying to push a new development model that's a lot more responsible and sustainable."

A 'more responsible and sustainable' model for Hennig's team, as Kittleson explains it, meant making a decision early on that was committed to a much longer-term plan, to avoid the kind of pitfalls that have helped make the video game industry as volatile as it currently is.

"When we see all of the disruption in the industry over the last few years, we don't want to replicate that by recreating all of the same problems," Kittleson said. "So you can build a team or you can build a game, but you can rarely build a team and a game. Skydance New Media started from scratch during the pandemic and had to build the team and had to build their tools, and then they got to work building the game."

"And I think there were really ambitious timelines set for that game, but they didn't take into account all of the things that can disrupt production and that can make you spend more time working on it. So there was a decision made that rather than try to push the game out as quickly as possible, it would be better to focus on the quality of the game and making sure the team has time to cook. So we're giving them the time to cook. We believe in Amy and the whole team there and supporting them, and that game will come out when it's ready. But it is very real and we are continuing to invest in it."

When pressed to put a date to what 'time to cook' means, Kittleson replied, "I don't think it'll be 2026," confirming that it'll be 2027 before the game might be in our hands. That makes it five years between when Hennig first teased this project and three years between when it was officially revealed.

At its 2024 reveal, it was given a 2025 release window, which was then pushed back to 2026 late last year. Kittleson's 2027 confirmation gives the game its second public delay, and it wouldn't be surprising if it's not the last one before we are finally able to play the first new game led by Hennig since 2011.

"We want to make sure that the team at New Media under Amy is set up for a great future," Kittleson continued, "and that means making sure that their debut game hits with maximum force. Better to give them time, better to give them the resources they need and make the game that really brings Amy's vision to life."

So what about that Star Wars game? "I think the best thing to think of is we've got to finish Marvel 1943," Kittleson said. "We're very much focused on that. So one thing at a time and we'll address Star Wars, talk about that when 1943 is finished. But first right now, all efforts are on 1943." Kittleson also didn't say one way or another if the Star Wars game was a lock as still happening after Marvel 1943. "That's something we'll have to talk about after 1943."

So while Kittleson isn't wrong that it's best to ensure Hennig and her team have a real grand slam on their hands rather than rush something out, the pressure it puts on Rise of Hydra to succeed only skyrockets from here. Paramount will need to see a return on all of this development time, after all. Hopefully, it'll be up to the task by the time it's out.

About the author: David has been writing about videogames, technology, and culture since 2020, with a focus on reporting daily news across multiple publications, including GameDaily.Biz, GameSkinny, and PlayStation Universe before joining Wccftech in 2025. David started contributing as Canada/US reporter for Wccftech's gaming section in 2025. Besides being up-to-date on the industry's movements, he loves interviewing developers, reviewing games, and writing intricate essays about the symbolism and layered meanings to be found in rich narratives as he's done for publications like GamesIndustry.Biz, LostInCult, and others. Outside of games he loves movies, music, theatre, his hometown, and his family, though not necessarily in that order.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.

item.title: Moss: The Forgotten Relic is a Much-Needed VR-Free Re-Release
item.content:encoded:
While VR has always been a great technology for enthusiasts, it does present something of a barrier to entry that can be insurmountable for some. That’s why it’s always good to see previously VR-only titles getting a VR-free release, and why I was so happy to be offered a chance for this Moss: The Forgotten Relic preview. It’s finally a chance to enjoy the mouse-centric 3D platformer without a television strapped directly to my head.

If you’re not lucky enough to own a VR device yourself, or you’ve just missed the original release, the Moss is a series of 3D platformers where you help a small adventurous mouse called Quill through a series of fantasy-themed adventures. Along the way, you personally take part in the story as ‘The Reader’, a human-sized entity who can interact with various elements in the world to help out your tiny companion. Moss: The Forgotten Relic is a non-VR re-release of both Moss and Moss: Book II that brings the series to the Switch and Xbox platforms for the first time, to boot.

The elements of the games are still there, with the only difference being the lack of any VR or motion-controller support. You’ve still got to interact with the world to make it more passible for Quill, but now all of those controls are simply mapped to the right-side of the controller. I was given the opportunity to sit down with the game’s demo before its release later this year to get a vertical slice of the Moss: Book II half of the game.

What’s Different

While I may not have played the specific part of the game that I was demoing before, I do have a decent amount of experience with the original Moss thanks to PSVR, so I was reasonably sure I knew what to expect from this release. Moss: The Forgotten Relic provides you with all of the jumping, combat, and puzzles of the original games, but without the ability to control your view by moving your head around.

From the outset, the vibe is very subdued, both stylistically and mechanically. You have the ability to heal Quill whenever you want, so there’s not much danger outside of getting swarmed in combat, at least, during normal gameplay. You’re mostly free to enjoy the environments and take your time trying to solve the puzzles, which is great because the inability to move your head around the environments does mean you’ll need to spend more time hunting for collectibles if you’re that way inclined.

Adorable & Explorable

Even with such a small slice of the game, it’s easy to see why it’s great that Moss is getting a non-VR release at last. Quill is possibly one of the cutest characters in gaming, and the unique world-interaction mechanics mean that there’s always something interesting going on. It’s also good that the preview showed off the Book II half of Moss: The Forgotten Relic, because the combat has also been expanded over what was on offer in the first game, even including a thrilling-looking boss fight right at the end of my preview time.

While the base combat can get a bit same-y after a while, the great variety of puzzles and the updates to combat do promise a deeper 3D platformer experience. The biggest concern is simply whether or not the collectibles are going to be semi-impossible to find without being able to visually scan the environment outside of VR. Though there’s every chance that the developers will think of a way around it before the final release, and to look at it another way, it means you really have to dig into the lush environments a bit more. 

Final Thoughts

Though my time with it was all too brief, Moss: The Forgotten Relic is a much-awaited re-release that seems like it’s going to finally provide everyone with a way of enjoying this adorable animal adventure, VR-owner or not. While some of the ‘wow’ factor might be missing along with the VR support, the gameplay is still as worthwhile and enjoyable an experience as it was before. Just keep an extra eye out for those collectibles in case you end up missing a bunch of them behind walls or corners.

Moss: The Forgotten Relic was previewed on PC via Steam with a copy provided by the publisher over the course of 30 Minutes - All images were taken during the course of this Preview.

Will has been writing about video games professionally since 2016 and has covered everything from AAA game reviews to industry events and everything in… More about William

item.title: All Confirmed & Rumored Games For June 9 Nintendo Direct
item.content:encoded:
The showcase season is beginning to come to a close, with most of the heavy hitters having had their time in the spotlight. However, there’s one big name left, and that’s Nintendo, with a Direct now confirmed for June 9 at 7 AM PT.

So far, it’s generally been an incredible Summer for gamers, with a wealth of new announcements across the board. That said, the fun isn’t over yet, as Nintendo are set to round it all out—but what games can we expect to see during the Direct?

It’s a valid question, as Nintendo have been as elusive as ever, but we’ve put together a list of what you can expect to see during the June 9 Nintendo Direct—check them all out below:

Every Confirmed Game For The Nintendo Direct

As things stand, no games have been confirmed to appear at the June 9 Nintendo Direct, though there are a few safe bets.

The obvious title that should appear during the June 9 Nintendo Direct is Star Fox, the remake of the classic Nintendo 64 game. With a release date of June 25, it’s likely a new trailer will appear to get fans hyped one last time before the game’s back in our hands.

  • More Insider Gaming: Summer Game Fest 2026 Showcase—Everything Announced

As for other games, we’ll probably see more of Splatoon Raiders and Fire Emblem Shadows, with both games due to be released in the coming months. As for everything else? That’s where the speculating begins…

Every Rumored Game For The Nintendo Direct

There’s room for plenty of surprises at this Nintendo Direct, whether it be new ports of beloved titles or entirely new announcements. Here’s what we think has a strong chance of appearing based on rumors and speculation.

Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time Remake

This will most likely be one of the heavy hitters for the showcase, and it’s long been rumored that it’s in development. With a supposed release window of Christmas this year, an official reveal would go down a treat.

3D Mario

Mario has been killing it at the box office lately, but he’s long overdue for a return to the 3D gaming space. According to reports, the next 3D Mario does exist, but it’s not due until 2027. That said, could we still see it announced this year, with gameplay to follow in the future? Fans wishing for Mario Odyssey 2 would certainly hope so.

New Switch Sports Title

The Wii Sports era will forever remain unmatched, but there are rumors circulating that a new Switch Sports title is in the works. The last one was a fine outing, if not slightly underwhelming, but with the capabilities of the Switch 2, it feels like it’s only a matter of time before a comprehensive sports title takes off again.

The Duskbloods

The Duskbloods is a Nintendo exclusive FromSoftware game that has plenty of fans excited, though the release date for the 2026-slated game still remains unknown. Could we get a better glimpse of the game during the Direct, as well as an idea of when we can finally get our hands on it? Fingers crossed!

Mario Kart World DLC

Mario Kart World has been out for roughly a year now, and that means it’s high time for some DLC. Whether that would take the form of new additions to its character roster or courses remains to be seen, but fans of the racer would undoubtedly take both.

Pokémon Pokopia DLC

Sticking with the DLC theme, there’s no denying that Pokopia was a smash hit, and many fans have been calling for new explorable areas where they can gather even more Pokémon. It feels like the possibilities are endless with this one, and it’s bound to happen at some point, so why not now?

Fire Emblem: Fortune’s Weave

The next instalment in the Fire Emblem franchise is slated for a 2026 release window, though little has been heard of the game since its announcement. It’s well overdue for an update, with a potential release date on the cards.

A Ton Of Switch 2 Third-Party Ports

The Nintendo Switch 2 collects ports like Pokémon, and throughout Summer Game Fest, a host of games have already been confirmed for the platform. Well, it likely won’t stop there, as some of the best games from the past few years are bound to arrive on the handheld, with a ton already leaked or rumored, including:

  • Clair Obscur: Expedition 33
  • Monster Hunter Wilds
  • Metaphor: ReFantazio
  • Mewgenics
  • Guardians of the Galaxy
  • Starfield
  • Stellar Blade

And so many more! It’s like shooting fish in a barrel trying to guess which ones could appear, so go in with an open mind!

Other Potential Appearances

As usual, there are bound to be plenty of surprises during the Nintendo Direct, and it’s impossible to predict them all. That said, here are a few more titles that could appear at the event, even if they’re along the lines of wishful thinking:

  • New Zelda title
  • Something Kirby (Remake?)
  • New WarioLand/WarioWare
  • Super Metroid Remake
  • Metroid 6
  • Rhythm Heaven Groove
  • New Xenoblade Chronicles

Are you excited for the Nintendo Direct? Do you think we’re missing any potential reveals? If so, let us know in the comments below, or share your pre-show predictions in the official Insider Gaming Discord Server.

For more Insider Gaming content, check out the following:

  • Insider Gaming Showcase—Everything Announced
  • XBOX Games Showcase 2026—Everything Announced
  • Sony State of Play June 2026 Showcase—Everything Announced
